title: 8.5 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 8.5!

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the Great Depression spread so quickly from the United States to other parts of the world?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: U.S. loans and investments propped up European economies, so the crash spread globally.
The US won the First World War, which meant it controlled the world’s economy.
Europe started an economic war with the United States that made the economy collapse.
Countries around the world stopped trading with the United States to protect their own economies.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was a major cause of the Great Depression?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Financial instability, risky investments, and unequal wealth distribution led to a collapse in the economy.
The U.S. economy never recovered after World War I, which left industries weak and unable to grow.
The threat of another global conflict caused panic in the markets and disrupted world trade.
Falling farm prices from overproduction caused widespread unemployment and a total economic collapse.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did many world governments respond to the Great Depression?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They closed off their economies from international trade.
They worked to make their economies more connected through trade agreements.
Some governments launched an economic war against the United States.
Colonies started producing fewer cash crops, which impacted economies in Europe.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main reason internationalism was seen as a threat to the fascist agenda? Choose the *best* answer.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Fascists needed to turn people away from ideas that promoted unity across nations.
Internationalism prevented fascists from uniting workers to overthrow capitalism.
Fascists opposed internationalism because they viewed other nations as racially inferior.
Internationalism was a nationalist ideology that took support away from fascism.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did fascist regimes see total war as the only kind of war worth fighting?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Fascists glorified war and rejected limits on their power to use them.
Fascists saw total war as a way to remove traditional elites from power.
Fascists used total war to promote and defend democratic values.
Fascists believed war could unify the state in service of the people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How do fascist leaders weaponize race to gain and maintain power?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They promote racial purity by blaming marginalized groups for national problems.
They encourage racial diversity as a strength of the nation.
They eliminate racial identity from political discussions to promote national unity.
They use racial categories mainly for scientific and academic purposes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How were the authoritarian regimes in Italy, Germany, Japan, and the Soviet Union similar during the 1920s and 1930s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They all used violence against civilians to achieve their goals.
They all adopted fascist systems of government to rule.
They all used race as a tool to control the population.
They all divided civilians based on class to achieve their goals.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Mussolini pursue an aggressive foreign policy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He attacked the Greek island of Corfu and supported violence in Libya.
He blamed Ukrainian Kulaks for the worsening situation in Italy.
He acted harshly towards his colonies in Namibia and in the Pacific Ocean.
He expanded into Korea, Mongolia, and Manchuria in East Asia.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How were German and Italian justifications for territorial expansions similar?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They both claimed that their nation needed more space for “pure” Germans and Italians.
They both wanted to confiscate land from peasant groups like the Kulaks.
They both faced shortages of natural resources in their territory.
They both believed in creating an international movement of workers and anti-racists.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the League of Nations ultimately fail to maintain peace in the 1930s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It couldn’t prevent aggressive powers like Italy, Japan, and Germany from invading other countries.
The United States and the Soviet Union joined but ended up fighting each other in the Cold War.
Britain and France did not have the power needed to address global conflicts.
The League did not include enough of the world’s countries to solve global problems.
It was an anti-war treaty between the United States and France.
It was a trade agreement between the United States and France.
It was a military alliance between the United States and France.
It was an anti-fascist policy between the United States and France.

assessment_items.question_content:
The cartoon below provides commentary on why the League of Nations failed. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What message is the political cartoon trying to convey about the League of Nations?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The image shows a bridge connecting two pieces of land. Each stone of the bridge is labeled with a member country of the league of nations -Belgium, France, England, Italy- with the middle stone missing, leaving a gap. On the left side of the bridge a sign reads, “The League of Nations Bridge was designed by the President of the United States”. On the right right of the bridge Uncle Sam is smoking a cigar and resting his head on the missing keystone of the bridge which reads, “USA”.
options.caption: “*The Gap in the Bridge*,” by Leonard Raven-Hill, public domain.

choices.content: The League of Nations was weak because the United States never joined.
The League of Nations was successful in uniting European nations after World War I.
The League of Nations focused too heavily on American interests at the expense of others.
The League of Nations was designed as a European alliance to exclude the United States.
